services.best_for: Owner-operators and small trades shops that want dead-simple, mobile, ADP-backed payroll with flat pricing and unlimited runs
services.weaknesses: Mobile-first design means a weaker web experience, it is payroll-focused rather than full HR, and it is a newer product. But the chat-based speed, flat unlimited-run pricing, and ADP tax backing are standout strengths.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: positive — praised as fast, simple mobile payroll at a reasonable cost; high app-store ratings; some note a dated web UI

services.affiliate_payout_note: No public publisher affiliate program confirmed — outreach required (ADP product).

service_faqs.q: How much does Roll by ADP cost?
service_faqs.a: A flat $39/month for unlimited payroll runs plus $5 per employee, with the first three months free for new customers and no long-term contract. The unlimited runs are valuable for trades that pay weekly or run off-cycle.

service_faqs.q: How does Roll by ADP work?
service_faqs.a: It is a chat-based, mobile-first app — you run payroll by typing a natural-language command like "run payroll," and Roll (backed by ADP) calculates pay and files/pays payroll taxes automatically. Owners report running payroll in about five minutes.

service_faqs.q: Is Roll by ADP good for small contractors?
service_faqs.a: Yes, especially owner-operators who manage the business from a phone and value simplicity and unlimited payroll runs. The trade-offs are a weaker desktop experience and a payroll focus without deep HR or scheduling.

service_faqs.q: Roll by ADP vs Gusto or Homebase?
service_faqs.a: Roll wins on mobile simplicity and flat unlimited-run pricing. Gusto offers more full-featured HR, benefits, and a richer web product; Homebase adds scheduling and time tracking for hourly crews. Choose Roll for frictionless mobile payroll, the others for broader HR/workforce features.

services.name: Wave Payroll
services.tagline: Low-cost payroll that plugs into Wave's free accounting — simple and affordable for tiny shops
services.pricing_summary: $20/month + $6 per active employee/contractor (self-service: you file taxes), or $40/month + $6 per active employee/contractor (full-service with automatic tax filing in all 50 states).
services.long_description:
Wave Payroll is the payroll module of Wave, the long-running small-business financial suite known for its genuinely free accounting and invoicing. Founded in 2010 in Toronto and acquired by H&R Block in 2019, Wave's pitch to a tiny trades shop is integration and price: run your books on Wave's free accounting, then bolt on payroll so wages, taxes, and expenses flow into the same ledger automatically. For a solo operator or micro-shop that already wants free invoicing and accounting, adding payroll in the same place is the path of least resistance.

Pricing is straightforward and cheap. After Wave's 2025 plan change, payroll is $20/month plus $6 per active employee or contractor on a self-service basis (you handle tax filings), or $40/month plus $6 per employee/contractor for full-service with automatic tax calculation, filing, and payment in all 50 states. Either way it is among the lower-cost options, and the tight link to Wave's free accounting is the real differentiator.

Reviewers (G2 around 4.1) praise the easy-to-use interface, the automated tax filing on the full-service plan, and the seamless accounting integration. The recurring gripes are a clunky two-step process for paying 1099 contractors and no support for international payroll — both fine for a typical US trades shop with W-2 employees, but worth knowing.

For a very small trades business — especially a solo operator or micro-shop already using or open to Wave's free accounting — Wave Payroll is a low-cost, simple way to add compliant payroll without a separate system. Larger shops or those wanting deep HR, scheduling, or benefits will outgrow it and should look at Gusto or Homebase.
services.how_it_works: If you use Wave's (free) accounting, you add the Payroll module; you can also use Payroll standalone. You set up employees/contractors and pay schedules, and run payroll online — wages, taxes, and expenses post automatically into Wave's accounting ledger. On the full-service plan ($40/mo + $6/employee), Wave calculates, files, and pays payroll taxes in all 50 states; on the self-service plan ($20/mo + $6/employee) you handle the filings. Direct deposit and employee self-service are included. The defining feature is the tight integration with Wave's free accounting and invoicing.

pros_detail.title: Low cost
pros_detail.explanation: From $20/mo + $6/employee (self-service) or $40/mo + $6/employee (full-service tax filing), Wave Payroll is among the cheapest compliant payroll options — ideal for solo operators and micro-shops.

pros_detail.title: Seamless Wave accounting integration
pros_detail.explanation: Wages, taxes, and expenses flow straight into Wave's free accounting ledger, so a tiny shop can run books and payroll in one place without manual journal entries.

pros_detail.title: Automatic tax filing (full-service)
pros_detail.explanation: The $40/mo plan calculates, files, and pays payroll taxes automatically in all 50 states — hands-off compliance for a low price.

pros_detail.title: Easy to use
pros_detail.explanation: Reviewers (G2 ~4.1) consistently praise the simple, clean interface and straightforward payroll process — well-suited to non-experts.

pros_detail.title: H&R Block-backed
pros_detail.explanation: Wave has been owned by H&R Block since 2019, adding tax pedigree and stability to the platform behind your payroll.

cons_detail.title: Clunky 1099 contractor process
cons_detail.explanation: Reviewers note paying 1099 contractors is a two-step process — a minor friction for trades that lean heavily on subcontractors.

cons_detail.title: No international payroll
cons_detail.explanation: Wave Payroll is US (and Canada) focused with no international payroll support — fine for typical US trades, but a hard limit if you ever need it.

cons_detail.title: Self-service plan offloads tax filing
cons_detail.explanation: The cheapest $20/mo plan does not file taxes for you. Hands-off filing requires the $40/mo full-service plan, so compare the real cost.

cons_detail.title: Best for very small shops
cons_detail.explanation: Its strength is simplicity and price for solo/micro operations. Growing shops needing deep HR, benefits, or scheduling will outgrow it.

cons_detail.title: Value tied to Wave ecosystem
cons_detail.explanation: The biggest advantage is integration with Wave accounting; if you do not use Wave for books, some of the appeal is lost versus a standalone payroll specialist.

services.best_for: Solo operators and micro trades shops — especially those using Wave's free accounting — that want simple, low-cost, compliant payroll in the same place as their books
services.weaknesses: A two-step 1099 process, no international payroll, and a cheapest plan that offloads tax filing. But the low price and seamless Wave-accounting integration make it strong for very small shops.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: positive on price and Wave-accounting integration; gripes about the 1099 two-step process and no international payroll

services.affiliate_payout_note: No public publisher affiliate program confirmed — outreach required (H&R Block / Wave).

service_faqs.q: How much does Wave Payroll cost?
service_faqs.a: $20/month plus $6 per active employee or contractor on a self-service basis (you file taxes), or $40/month plus $6 per employee/contractor for full-service with automatic tax filing in all 50 states. It is among the cheapest compliant payroll options.

service_faqs.q: Do I need Wave accounting to use Wave Payroll?
service_faqs.a: No, but the main advantage is the seamless integration — if you use Wave's free accounting, wages, taxes, and expenses post automatically to your ledger. Standalone use works, but you lose part of the appeal.

service_faqs.q: Does Wave Payroll file my payroll taxes?
service_faqs.a: On the full-service plan ($40/mo + $6/employee) it calculates, files, and pays payroll taxes automatically in all 50 states. On the cheaper self-service plan ($20/mo + $6/employee), you handle the filings yourself.

service_faqs.q: What are Wave Payroll's limitations?
service_faqs.a: Paying 1099 contractors is a two-step process, there is no international payroll, and it is best suited to very small shops. Growing trades businesses that need deep HR, benefits, or scheduling will outgrow it.

services.name: Homebase
services.tagline: All-in-one team management for hourly crews — scheduling, time clock, and payroll in one app
services.pricing_summary: Team-management tiers per location/month: Basic (free), Essentials $24.95, Plus $56, All-in-One $96. Payroll is an add-on at $39/month + $6 per employee. No setup, integration, or per-user fees.
services.long_description:
Homebase is a workforce-management platform built for small businesses that run hourly teams — and that makes it an unusually good fit for trades crews. Founded in 2014 in San Francisco, it combines employee scheduling, time tracking, team messaging, hiring and onboarding, HR/compliance, and payroll in a single app, with over 100,000 customers and consistently high app-store ratings (4.8 on iOS across 90,000+ reviews). For a contractor managing a rotating crew of technicians or laborers, the scheduling-plus-time-clock-plus-payroll combination tackles the actual daily pain: who is working, did they clock in, and did the hours flow correctly into pay.

Its pricing is genuinely accessible. The team-management platform has a free Basic tier, then Essentials ($24.95), Plus ($56), and All-in-One ($96) per location per month — and Homebase is transparent that it charges no setup, integration, or per-user fees. Payroll is a separate add-on at $39/month plus $6 per employee. So a trades shop can start free for scheduling and time tracking, then layer on payroll when ready, paying for a location rather than per head on the base tiers.

The honest caveat is that Homebase's payroll module is widely seen as the weaker half of the product — reviewers (and titles like "good for scheduling, weak on payroll") note the payroll add-on is less mature than dedicated payroll platforms, the mobile app can be slow or crash during heavy shift management, and its integrations with external payroll/accounting are less comprehensive than competitors. Where Homebase shines is the scheduling and time-tracking core; payroll is a convenient add-on, not a best-in-class engine.

For a trades business with hourly crews whose biggest headache is scheduling and accurate time tracking, Homebase is one of the best-fit tools in this directory — and bundling payroll keeps everything in one place. Shops whose priority is the most robust payroll itself may pair Homebase scheduling with a dedicated payroll platform (Gusto integrates with Homebase) rather than using Homebase payroll alone.
services.how_it_works: You set up your location, employees, and schedules in Homebase; employees clock in/out via the time clock (with GPS and photo options), and tracked hours flow into timesheets. You can message the team, post schedules, and manage hiring and onboarding in the same app. Adding the payroll module ($39/mo + $6/employee) turns approved timesheets into a payroll run that calculates wages and taxes, files payroll taxes, and pays employees via direct deposit. The base team-management tiers (free Basic up to $96 All-in-One) are priced per location with no per-user fees. Homebase also integrates with external payroll (e.g., Gusto) if you prefer to keep payroll elsewhere.

pros_detail.title: Built for hourly crews
pros_detail.explanation: Scheduling, time clock, and team messaging are tailored to hourly workforces — exactly how most trades crews operate — so daily workforce management is genuinely easier.

pros_detail.title: Free Basic tier, no per-user fees
pros_detail.explanation: A genuinely useful free plan plus per-location (not per-user) pricing on base tiers makes Homebase accessible for small shops; you can start free and add payroll later.

pros_detail.title: Time tracking flows into payroll
pros_detail.explanation: Approved timesheets feed the payroll add-on directly, reducing manual re-entry and timesheet-to-paycheck errors — a real win for crews paid on tracked hours.

pros_detail.title: All-in-one workforce app
pros_detail.explanation: Scheduling, time tracking, messaging, hiring, onboarding, HR, and payroll in one app — covering more of the hourly-workforce workflow than most competitors in its price range.

pros_detail.title: Strong usability and ratings
pros_detail.explanation: 100,000+ customers and a 4.8 iOS rating across 90,000+ reviews reflect a genuinely easy-to-use product that crews actually adopt.

cons_detail.title: Payroll is the weaker half
cons_detail.explanation: Reviewers consistently rate the payroll add-on below dedicated payroll platforms — fine as a convenience, but not best-in-class. Payroll-first shops may prefer a specialist.

cons_detail.title: Payroll is a paid add-on
cons_detail.explanation: Payroll costs an extra $39/mo + $6/employee on top of your team-management tier, so the all-in cost for scheduling plus payroll adds up versus a payroll-only tool.

cons_detail.title: Mobile app performance
cons_detail.explanation: Some users report the mobile app loading slowly or crashing during heavy shift management — frustrating when you are managing a crew in real time.

cons_detail.title: Limited external integrations
cons_detail.explanation: Integrations with outside payroll/accounting are less comprehensive than some competitors, so deeply integrated stacks may hit gaps.

cons_detail.title: Best value is the workforce side
cons_detail.explanation: Homebase earns its keep on scheduling and time tracking; if you do not need those, a dedicated payroll platform may be a better, cheaper fit for payroll alone.

services.best_for: Trades businesses with hourly crews whose biggest pain is scheduling and accurate time tracking, and who want payroll bundled into the same app
services.weaknesses: The payroll add-on is less mature than dedicated payroll platforms, payroll costs extra on top of the team-management tier, and the mobile app can lag. But for hourly-crew scheduling and time tracking, it is one of the best fits — and bundling payroll is convenient.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: positive — loved for hourly scheduling and time tracking; payroll add-on seen as convenient but weaker than dedicated payroll tools

services.affiliate_payout_note: No public publisher affiliate program confirmed — outreach required to determine availability.

service_faqs.q: How much does Homebase cost?
service_faqs.a: The team-management platform has a free Basic tier, then Essentials ($24.95), Plus ($56), and All-in-One ($96) per location per month, with no setup, integration, or per-user fees. Payroll is a separate add-on at $39/month plus $6 per employee.

service_faqs.q: Is Homebase good for trades crews?
service_faqs.a: Yes — it is built for hourly workforces, so scheduling, time clock, and team messaging fit how trades crews operate. Tracked hours flow into the payroll add-on, reducing timesheet-to-paycheck errors. It is one of the best-fit workforce tools for crew-based shops.

service_faqs.q: Is Homebase payroll any good?
service_faqs.a: It is convenient because it is bundled with scheduling and time tracking, but reviewers consistently rate the payroll add-on below dedicated payroll platforms. If payroll robustness is your top priority, you can use Homebase for scheduling/time tracking and integrate a dedicated payroll tool like Gusto.

service_faqs.q: Can I start Homebase for free?
service_faqs.a: Yes — the Basic team-management tier is free and genuinely useful for scheduling and time tracking. You can add paid tiers and the payroll module ($39/mo + $6/employee) as your needs grow.

services.name: Paycor
services.tagline: Mid-market HCM with payroll, HR, and timekeeping for growing trades operations
services.pricing_summary: Per-employee-per-month, typically ~$19-$27 PEPM depending on company size and modules. No public rate card — sales-led, custom-quoted, varies with employee count, module mix, states, and implementation scope.
services.long_description:
Paycor is a mid-market human-capital-management (HCM) platform that has provided cloud payroll, HR, and timekeeping since 1990 out of Cincinnati. Where SMB tools like Gusto or SurePayroll fit a small shop, Paycor is built for the trades business that has outgrown basic payroll — one with real headcount, multiple roles, recruiting needs, and a desire for analytics on labor cost and turnover. It bundles payroll with HR, timekeeping, onboarding, talent management, and reporting in one system.

For a growing contractor, the appeal is consolidation and insight: instead of separate payroll, time-tracking, and HR tools, Paycor unifies them, and its analytics are a frequently-praised strength for owners who want to understand labor cost and workforce trends. It scales the people-operations side of the business as crews and office staff grow.

The trade-offs are mid-market ones. Paycor does not publish a public rate card — pricing is sales-led and runs roughly $19-$27 per employee per month depending on size, modules, and states, so you go through sales rather than a transparent online sign-up. Reviews are solid but split on support (Capterra ~4.4, G2 ~3.9), with some users citing slow customer service. It is also more platform than a very small shop needs.

For an established, growing trades business that wants HCM-grade payroll plus HR, timekeeping, and analytics in one system, Paycor is a credible mid-market choice. Small shops should stay with SMB payroll (Gusto, SurePayroll); fast-growing operations comparing mid-market HCM should weigh Paycor against Paylocity and Rippling.
services.how_it_works: Paycor is sold through a sales process (no public self-serve rate card). After scoping your employee count, module needs (payroll, HR, timekeeping, onboarding, talent), and states, you get a per-employee-per-month quote (~$19-$27 PEPM). Implementation migrates your payroll, employee, and time data, then your team runs payroll and manages HR/timekeeping in the unified platform, with analytics on labor cost and workforce trends. It is built for mid-market operations rather than instant small-shop setup.

pros_detail.title: Unified HCM for growing shops
pros_detail.explanation: Payroll, HR, timekeeping, onboarding, and talent management in one platform, so a growing trades business consolidates people operations instead of stitching separate tools together.

pros_detail.title: Strong analytics
pros_detail.explanation: Reviewers specifically praise Paycor's analytics — useful for an owner who wants visibility into labor cost, turnover, and workforce trends as the team scales.

pros_detail.title: Built for mid-market scale
pros_detail.explanation: Founded in 1990, Paycor is designed for businesses with real headcount and multiple roles, with the depth a shop needs after outgrowing basic SMB payroll.

pros_detail.title: Timekeeping built in
pros_detail.explanation: Integrated timekeeping ties hours to payroll within the same system — valuable for trades with hourly field staff and overtime to manage.

pros_detail.title: Recruiting and onboarding
pros_detail.explanation: Talent and onboarding tools help a growing trades operation hire and ramp crew and office staff, beyond just running payroll.

cons_detail.title: No public pricing
cons_detail.explanation: Paycor has no public rate card; pricing is sales-led (~$19-$27 PEPM) and varies by size, modules, and states, so you go through sales rather than a transparent online sign-up.

cons_detail.title: Mixed support reviews
cons_detail.explanation: Ratings are solid but split (Capterra ~4.4, G2 ~3.9), with some users citing slow customer service — worth probing references on support responsiveness.

cons_detail.title: More platform than small shops need
cons_detail.explanation: Its HCM depth fits mid-market operations; a very small trades shop will find it heavier and pricier than SMB payroll like Gusto or SurePayroll.

cons_detail.title: Implementation effort
cons_detail.explanation: As an HCM platform, onboarding involves data migration and configuration across payroll, HR, and timekeeping — a real implementation, not a five-minute setup.

cons_detail.title: Module-based cost
cons_detail.explanation: Cost depends on which modules you enable, so the full HCM value comes at the higher end of the PEPM range — scope carefully to control spend.

services.best_for: Established, growing trades businesses with real headcount that want HCM-grade payroll plus HR, timekeeping, and analytics in one platform
services.weaknesses: No public pricing, mixed support reviews, and more platform than a small shop needs. But for a growing trades operation that has outgrown SMB payroll, its unified HCM and strong analytics are a solid mid-market fit.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: mid-market HCM regarded for analytics and breadth; some complaints about support responsiveness and sales-led pricing

services.affiliate_payout_note: No public publisher affiliate program confirmed — outreach required to determine availability.

service_faqs.q: How much does Paycor cost?
service_faqs.a: Paycor does not publish a public rate card. Pricing is per-employee-per-month, typically ~$19-$27 PEPM depending on company size and modules, and is sales-led — you get a custom quote based on headcount, module mix, states, and implementation scope.

service_faqs.q: Is Paycor good for a trades business?
service_faqs.a: For a growing operation with real headcount, yes — it unifies payroll, HR, timekeeping, onboarding, and analytics. A very small shop is better served by SMB payroll (Gusto, SurePayroll); Paycor's value shows once you have outgrown basic payroll and want HCM depth.

service_faqs.q: Paycor vs Paylocity vs Rippling?
service_faqs.a: All are mid-market HCM options. Paycor is praised for analytics; Paylocity for a clean modern interface; Rippling for IT/HR automation breadth. None publish full public pricing. Compare on the modules you need, support reputation, and quoted PEPM for your headcount.

service_faqs.q: Does Paycor include time tracking?
service_faqs.a: Yes — Paycor includes timekeeping in its HCM platform, tying hours to payroll within the same system, which matters for trades with hourly field staff and overtime.

services.name: Paylocity
services.tagline: Modern mid-market HR and payroll platform with a clean interface for scaling teams
services.pricing_summary: Per-employee-per-month, typically ~$22-$32 PEPM depending on company size and modules. No public pricing — custom-quoted to your business needs.
services.long_description:
Paylocity is a mid-market HR and payroll platform, founded in 1997 near Chicago, known for a clean, modern interface and a broad HCM feature set — payroll, HR, benefits administration, talent, and employee self-service — aimed at businesses scaling past basic SMB payroll. For a trades operation that has grown into multiple departments, benefits, and HR complexity, Paylocity offers the depth to manage it without an enterprise-grade implementation.

Its most-cited strength is usability: reviewers find it easy to navigate, with a clean interface that simplifies HR and payroll tasks and strong employee self-service (a real plus for a distributed crew that wants to check pay stubs and PTO from their phones). It competes directly with Paycor and Rippling in the mid-market HCM tier.

The trade-offs mirror the category. Paylocity does not publish pricing — it is custom-quoted, typically ~$22-$32 per employee per month depending on size and modules — so you go through sales. And reviews are genuinely mixed on service: some customers call the support team "the BEST," while others describe a rough payroll-transition and support experience. As with any HCM migration, the implementation and your assigned support team make or break the early experience.

For a growing trades business that values a modern, easy-to-use HR/payroll platform with strong self-service, Paylocity is a strong mid-market option. Small shops should use SMB payroll; mid-market shops should trial Paylocity against Paycor and Rippling and probe support references before committing.
services.how_it_works: Paylocity is sold via a sales process with custom per-employee-per-month pricing (~$22-$32 PEPM, no public rate card). After scoping headcount and modules (payroll, HR, benefits, talent, self-service), implementation migrates your data and configures the platform. Your team then runs payroll and manages HR in a modern interface, while employees use self-service for pay stubs, PTO, and personal info. The early experience depends heavily on the implementation and assigned support team.

pros_detail.title: Mid-market fit

service_faqs.a: Paylocity does not publish pricing. It is per-employee-per-month, typically ~$22-$32 PEPM depending on company size and modules, and is custom-quoted — you go through sales to get a number for your headcount and feature needs.

services.name: TriNet
services.tagline: Professional Employer Organization (PEO) bundling payroll, benefits, HR, and workers' comp via co-employment
services.pricing_summary: PEO co-employment, quote-based per employee per month — commonly ~$100-$150 PEPM depending on benefits package, states, and service level. Bundles payroll, benefits, HR, compliance, and workers' comp.
services.long_description:
TriNet is a Professional Employer Organization (PEO), which is a fundamentally different model from a payroll tool: through co-employment, TriNet becomes the employer-of-record for HR purposes, pooling your employees with thousands of others to deliver enterprise-grade benefits, payroll, HR compliance, risk management, and workers' compensation that a small trades shop could not access on its own. Founded in 1988 and ESAC-accredited, it targets small and mid-size businesses that want to offer competitive benefits and offload HR/compliance burden as they scale.

For a growing trades business, the PEO value is real: access to better, cheaper group health benefits and 401(k) than a 20-person shop could negotiate alone, plus workers'-comp administration (pooled, often with pay-as-you-go), payroll, and a team handling compliance across states. For a contractor competing to recruit and retain skilled techs, the benefits leverage can be a genuine advantage.

The trade-offs are cost and fit. PEO pricing is high — commonly ~$100-$150 per employee per month — because it bundles benefits administration and risk pooling, not just payroll, and it is quote-based. Reviews note it suits new and scaling businesses but is poor value for very small shops (under ~5 employees) or those who just want cheap payroll. Co-employment also means giving up some HR control to the PEO.

For an established or scaling trades business that wants enterprise-grade benefits, pooled workers' comp, and offloaded HR/compliance — and is willing to pay PEO rates for it — TriNet is a strong, accredited option. Shops that only need payroll, or have very few employees, should use a payroll tool (Gusto, SurePayroll) instead; another PEO option already in this directory is Justworks.
services.how_it_works: TriNet operates as a PEO via co-employment: it becomes the employer-of-record for HR/benefits/payroll-tax purposes while you retain day-to-day control of your workers. Your employees are pooled with TriNet's broader base to access group benefits (health, 401(k)) and workers'-comp programs. TriNet runs payroll, administers benefits, handles HR compliance across states, and manages risk. Pricing is quote-based per employee per month (commonly ~$100-$150 PEPM), reflecting the bundled benefits and services — far more than a standalone payroll tool, but covering much more.

pros_detail.title: Enterprise-grade benefits for small shops
pros_detail.explanation: By pooling your employees with thousands of others, TriNet delivers group health and 401(k) benefits a small trades shop could not negotiate alone — a real recruiting and retention edge.

pros_detail.title: Bundled workers' comp and risk
pros_detail.explanation: Workers'-comp administration (often pay-as-you-go) and risk management are bundled in, valuable for trades where WC is mandatory and rates are exposure-sensitive.

pros_detail.title: Offloads HR and compliance
pros_detail.explanation: TriNet handles HR compliance across states as your employer-of-record for those purposes, removing a major burden as a trades business grows multi-state.

pros_detail.title: Accredited and established
pros_detail.explanation: Founded in 1988 and ESAC-accredited, TriNet is an established, vetted PEO — important when entrusting payroll, benefits, and compliance to a co-employer.

pros_detail.title: Good for scaling businesses
pros_detail.explanation: Reviewers recommend it for new and scaling businesses that want to offer competitive benefits and offload HR rather than build those functions in-house.

cons_detail.title: High PEPM cost
cons_detail.explanation: PEO pricing commonly runs ~$100-$150 per employee per month because it bundles benefits and risk pooling, not just payroll — far more than a standalone payroll tool.

cons_detail.title: Poor fit for very small shops
cons_detail.explanation: Reviews note TriNet is poor value for businesses under ~5 employees or those who just want cheap payroll. The PEO model pays off at scale, not for a tiny crew.

cons_detail.title: Co-employment trade-off
cons_detail.explanation: Becoming a co-employer means ceding some HR control to TriNet. Owners who want full control of HR processes may find the model constraining.

cons_detail.title: Quote-based pricing
cons_detail.explanation: Pricing is custom and benefits-package-dependent, so you must go through sales and compare PEO quotes carefully (against Justworks and others).

cons_detail.title: Overkill if you only need payroll
cons_detail.explanation: If you just need to run payroll and file taxes, a PEO is far more (and more expensive) than necessary — use a payroll tool like Gusto or SurePayroll instead.

services.best_for: Established or scaling trades businesses that want enterprise-grade benefits, pooled workers' comp, and offloaded HR/compliance via a PEO, and will pay PEO rates for it
services.weaknesses: High PEPM cost, poor value under ~5 employees, and the co-employment control trade-off. But for a scaling trades business wanting competitive benefits, pooled workers' comp, and offloaded HR/compliance, TriNet is a strong, accredited PEO.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: recommended for new and scaling businesses wanting benefits and HR offload via a PEO; seen as poor value for very small shops or those who just want cheap payroll

service_faqs.q: What is a PEO and how does TriNet work?
service_faqs.a: A PEO (Professional Employer Organization) becomes the employer-of-record for HR purposes via co-employment, pooling your employees with thousands of others to deliver enterprise-grade benefits, payroll, HR compliance, and workers' comp. TriNet (founded 1988, ESAC-accredited) runs all of that while you keep day-to-day control of your workers.

service_faqs.q: How much does TriNet cost?
service_faqs.a: PEO pricing is quote-based, commonly ~$100-$150 per employee per month, because it bundles benefits administration and risk pooling — not just payroll. That is far more than a standalone payroll tool, but it covers benefits, workers' comp, HR, and compliance.

service_faqs.q: Is TriNet worth it for a trades business?
service_faqs.a: For an established or scaling shop that wants competitive group benefits, pooled workers' comp, and offloaded HR/compliance, yes. For a business under ~5 employees or one that just wants cheap payroll, a PEO is overkill — use a payroll tool like Gusto or SurePayroll instead.

service_faqs.q: TriNet vs Justworks?
service_faqs.a: Both are PEOs offering co-employment, benefits, payroll, and HR. TriNet is a large, long-established PEO with deep benefits and risk programs; Justworks is known for a simpler, more transparent experience. Compare quotes, benefits packages, and service models for your headcount and states.

services.name: Fingercheck
services.tagline: All-in-one payroll, time tracking, and HR built for construction and field crews — with certified payroll
services.pricing_summary: From about $5 per user per month (plus a base fee), with a free trial. Tiered by features; construction/field capabilities (GPS time, job costing, certified payroll) are a core part of the value.
services.long_description:
Fingercheck is an all-in-one payroll, time-tracking, and HR platform that is unusually well-suited to trades and construction because it was built around the field-crew reality: hours happen at job sites, not at a desk. Founded in 2013 in Brooklyn, it connects time tracking, scheduling, and payroll in a single native system, with GPS-verified time entries that automatically allocate hours to the correct job codes — giving a contractor real-time labor-cost tracking and project-level profitability rather than a month-end surprise.

For trades, two capabilities stand out. First, job costing: GPS-verified hours flowing to job codes means you can see budget-vs-actual labor by project as it happens. Second, certified payroll: Fingercheck generates the certified-payroll reports required on government/prevailing-wage construction projects, which is a genuine compliance headache it removes — and a reason construction shops specifically choose it. Overtime and break rules are calculated automatically in line with local law, and verified hours flow straight into payroll.

Pricing is accessible (from about $5 per user per month plus a base fee, with a free trial), and it is well-rated by users. The main considerations are that, as a specialized all-in-one, you are adopting it as your combined time/payroll/HR system rather than bolting payroll onto an existing tool, and feature tiers determine which construction capabilities you get — confirm certified payroll and job costing are in your plan if you need them.

For a construction or field-heavy trades business that wants time tracking, job costing, certified payroll, and payroll in one native system, Fingercheck is one of the best-fit options in this directory — more trades-native than generalist payroll tools. Office-only or very simple shops may not need its field depth and could use a simpler payroll tool.
services.how_it_works: Field workers clock in/out with GPS-verified time entries (mobile), and Fingercheck automatically allocates those hours to the correct job codes and calculates overtime/breaks per local law. Verified hours flow directly into payroll within the same native system — no re-entry between time tracking and pay. The platform also handles HR and generates certified-payroll reports for government/prevailing-wage projects. Pricing is per user (from ~$5/user/mo plus a base fee) with a free trial; feature tiers determine which construction capabilities (job costing, certified payroll) are included.

pros_detail.title: GPS time tracking to job codes
pros_detail.explanation: GPS-verified field time entries automatically allocate hours to the right job codes, giving real-time labor-cost and budget-vs-actual visibility by project — a core trades need most payroll tools miss.

pros_detail.title: Certified payroll built in
pros_detail.explanation: Fingercheck generates certified-payroll reports required on government/prevailing-wage construction projects, removing a real compliance headache and a key reason construction shops choose it.

pros_detail.title: Native time-to-payroll flow
pros_detail.explanation: Time tracking, scheduling, and payroll are one native system, so verified hours flow straight into pay with overtime/break rules auto-calculated — no error-prone re-entry between tools.

pros_detail.title: Affordable and well-rated
pros_detail.explanation: Starting around $5 per user per month (plus base) with a free trial, Fingercheck is accessible, and it earns strong user ratings for its all-in-one field/payroll approach.

pros_detail.title: Trades/construction-native
pros_detail.explanation: Built around field-crew reality rather than office workers, it fits how trades actually operate — job sites, hourly crews, and project labor costing.

cons_detail.title: Adopt it as your whole system
cons_detail.explanation: As a specialized all-in-one, Fingercheck works best when it is your combined time/payroll/HR system, not payroll bolted onto an existing tool — so switching means migrating those functions together.

cons_detail.title: Feature tiers gate capabilities
cons_detail.explanation: Construction capabilities like job costing and certified payroll depend on your plan tier; confirm the features you need are included rather than assuming the base plan has them.

cons_detail.title: Base fee on top of per-user
cons_detail.explanation: Pricing is ~$5/user/mo plus a base fee, so model the all-in cost for your crew size rather than just the per-user figure.

cons_detail.title: Overkill for office-only shops
cons_detail.explanation: Its field depth (GPS, job costing, certified payroll) is wasted on an office-only or very simple operation, which could use a simpler payroll tool more cheaply.

cons_detail.title: Smaller than the HCM giants
cons_detail.explanation: Fingercheck is a focused specialist, not a mid-market HCM; very large operations wanting deep HR/talent suites may compare it against Paycor/Paylocity.

services.best_for: Construction and field-heavy trades businesses that want GPS time tracking, job costing, certified payroll, and payroll in one native system
services.weaknesses: Best adopted as your whole time/payroll/HR system (not a bolt-on), construction features are tier-gated, and it is overkill for office-only shops. But for field crews needing job costing and certified payroll, it is one of the most trades-native options available.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: positive — valued by construction/field businesses for GPS job-costing, certified payroll, and native time-to-payroll flow

services.affiliate_payout_note: No public publisher affiliate program confirmed — outreach required to determine availability.

service_faqs.q: Is Fingercheck good for construction?
service_faqs.a: Yes — it is one of the most construction-native options here. GPS-verified time entries allocate hours to job codes for real-time labor costing, and it generates certified-payroll reports for government/prevailing-wage projects, with time tracking, scheduling, and payroll in one native system.

service_faqs.q: How much does Fingercheck cost?
service_faqs.a: From about $5 per user per month plus a base fee, with a free trial. Pricing is tiered, and construction capabilities like job costing and certified payroll depend on your plan, so confirm the features you need are included and model the all-in cost for your crew.

service_faqs.q: What is certified payroll and does Fingercheck handle it?
service_faqs.a: Certified payroll is the specialized reporting required on government/prevailing-wage construction projects. Fingercheck generates these reports, removing a significant compliance burden — a key reason construction contractors choose it over generalist payroll tools.

service_faqs.q: Fingercheck vs a generalist payroll tool?
service_faqs.a: Generalist tools (Gusto, SurePayroll) run payroll well but lack GPS job costing and certified payroll. Fingercheck is built for field crews, tying GPS-verified hours to job codes and pay. Field/construction shops lean Fingercheck; office-only or very simple operations may prefer a simpler generalist tool.
